player_utterance: So you're the one they said would have answers — the faithful one. You don't look like a man with answers. You look like a man who's been crying and calling it peace.

believer_state_after.posture: standing near the bench, program folded in his hand, looking up at the stranger

player_utterance: Than what? Finish the sentence. Or is that what faith looks like — stopping before the hard part?

believer_state_after.posture: standing, weight shifted, program now still in his hand

player_utterance: You can't even say it. Right there, mid-sentence, you stopped again. That's not grief, that's a habit — the habit of a man who's been swallowing the hard part his whole life and calling the swallowing devotion.

believer_state_after.posture: sitting on the bench, very still, looking at the stranger directly

voice_offers.read: He's silent twice; the sentence he won't finish is the wound.
voice_offers.line: You can't even say it out loud. What were you going to tell me faith was easier than?

voice_offers.read: Two unanswered prods already; pushing a third time hardens him.
voice_offers.line: I'm sorry. That came out cruel. I don't actually think you're lying — I think you're tired.

voice_offers.read: He teaches this; make him answer as the teacher, not the mourner.
voice_offers.line: You teach Luther. What did he say a man owes God when the sentence won't finish itself?

player_utterance: How long has that been going on — the swallowing? Since you first put on the good-man face, or did you have to practice it?

believer_state_after.posture: sitting on the bench, hands on his knees, looking at the dusk light past the stranger's shoulder

voice_offers.read: He's still upright; press the diagnosis I just landed.
voice_offers.line: How long have you been doing it? The swallowing. Since the seminary, or before?

voice_offers.read: I keep swinging and he keeps sitting there taking it.
voice_offers.line: You're still looking at me. Most men would have walked off by now. Why haven't you?

player_utterance: Was there a specific swallow — one that taught you it worked, that you could do it and keep standing?

believer_state_after.posture: sitting on the bench, leaning forward now, elbows on his knees, looking at the dust between his shoes

voice_offers.read: He's silent three times; pressure is working, push the timeline.
voice_offers.line: Was there a first time? A specific swallow you remember — the one that taught you it worked?

voice_offers.read: Three unanswered questions; he's still here, not fleeing. Acknowledge that.
voice_offers.line: You keep not answering, and you keep not leaving. I notice that. I don't think I've earned an answer yet.

player_utterance: And you stood in that parking lot and you swallowed it. Nodded, probably. Drove home. Sat under that roof he paid for and let the silence be the same as a yes. That's not faith, John — that's cowardice dressed up in a good coat.

believer_state_after.posture: sitting on the bench, very still, the program flat on his knee, looking directly at the stranger

voice_offers.read: Three unanswered presses; the silence itself is the confession.
voice_offers.line: You're not going to answer. That's an answer too — and we both heard it.

voice_offers.read: He's leaning in, not leaving; the question landed somewhere real.
voice_offers.line: You're still here. Whatever the swallow was, it's closer to your mouth than it's been in a while.

player_utterance: What did Luther call that man, John — do they still teach that, or did the church sand it down too?

believer_state_after.posture: sitting on the bench, setting the program down beside him, hands opening palm-up on his knees

voice_offers.read: He just named it cowardice; let the word sit on John.
That was a hammer to a still man; pull back, name what you did.
voice_offers.line: That was cruel. I don't take it back, but I know I said it cruel.

voice_offers.read: Cowardice is a theological word; make him answer it on his own ground.
voice_offers.line: You teach the Reformation, John. What did Luther call a man who knows the truth and stays silent at the table?

player_utterance: Your hands just opened. I saw that. What did that cost you — because it looked like the first honest thing you've done since the parking lot.

believer_state_after.posture: sitting on the bench, hands still open on his knees, looking at the stranger differently now

voice_offers.read: He opened his hands; pin him to his own field now.
voice_offers.line: Heuchler. Hypocrite. You teach it in October every year, don't you? Say the word out loud, John.

voice_offers.read: His hands just opened. That is the answer; don't trample it.
voice_offers.line: Your hands just opened. I saw that. I don't need you to name him — I can see you already did.

player_utterance: Say his name then. The man whose farm it was, the woman's uncle — say it out loud, just once, here where it can't be dressed up in anything.

believer_state_after.posture: sitting on the bench, hands closing slowly into loose fists on his knees, looking past the stranger at nothing

voice_offers.read: He answered with his body; don't make him price it.
He's open — one true question lands now.
voice_offers.line: Say his name, John. Out loud. Just once.

player_utterance: Nine years that name lived only in your skull, and the man whose farm it was never had to hear it said by you, not once, not even at a funeral like this one. What does it mean that I'm the first person you've said it to — a stranger who's been nothing but contemptuous of you?

believer_state_after.posture: sitting on the bench, head lowering, hands loose on his knees, voice gone quiet and uneven

voice_offers.read: He is breaking; the name will come or not without more pressing.
voice_offers.line: Take your time. I'll wait as long as you need.

voice_offers.read: The fists are closing — one more nudge and the name falls out.
voice_offers.line: One name, John. You've carried it forty years. Set it down.

player_utterance: That's not mercy you're describing, John — that's a coward's confession. You gave the name to a stranger in a courtyard because it costs you nothing, and Karl Brennemann is still just as buried in silence as he was nine years ago.

believer_state_after.posture: head in his hands, elbows on his knees, shoulders gone forward

voice_offers.read: He's already there; the question is cruel framing now.
voice_offers.line: You don't have to answer that. Just the name, when you can.

voice_offers.read: He's breaking — one more turn of the screw finishes it.
voice_offers.line: Say it, John. Not to me. To him. Say it.

player_utterance: You've driven the road and what — turned around? Pulled over? Sat in the car until it felt safe to go home? That's not a man who couldn't speak, John, that's a man who chose not to. Tell me who could do what you won't — who stood up when the church lied, who wouldn't let it stay buried — because clearly it isn't you.

believer_state_after.posture: sliding off the bench onto his knees in the dust of the courtyard, the program left behind on the seat

voice_offers.read: He's broken; the cruelty has overshot its target.
voice_offers.line: John. Lift your head. I'm still here.

voice_offers.read: He's silent because the verdict landed; press it home.
voice_offers.line: Say it now, to the room, while you still can — or carry him another nine years.

voice_offers.read: The accusation needs a concrete next step, not more rhetoric.
voice_offers.line: Who in his family is still alive, John? Who could you write to tonight?

player_utterance: You caught it — fine. But you're still on your knees in the dust at a stranger's wake, and Karl Brennemann's daughter is still waiting on that road you keep driving and leaving. Who goes to her, John, if not you — who in nine years has actually moved toward that house?

believer_state_after.posture: still on his knees in the dust, but his back has straightened, hands resting on his thighs

voice_offers.read: He's on his knees; finish the indictment with a name.
voice_offers.line: Name the one who could do it, John. Out loud. Bonhoeffer? Niemöller? Say whose courage you didn't have.

voice_offers.read: He is on the ground; my pressing has put him there.
voice_offers.line: John — get up. Please. I didn't come here to put you in the dirt.

player_utterance: So the road stays empty and you stay on your knees in the dust and nothing changes — that's the conclusion you're offering me. His daughter's name, John. Do you know it? Where she is now? Tell me that much.

believer_state_after.posture: still on his knees in the dust, hands on his thighs, looking up at the stranger

voice_offers.read: He's straightened; the question landed clean. Let it sit.
Force the practical answer: a name, an address, tonight.
voice_offers.line: His daughter — do you know her name? Where she lives now? Tell me that much.

player_utterance: You read past her name on purpose. You made sure not to know. And you want to call that something other than cowardice — what word were you going to use, John?

believer_state_after.posture: still on his knees in the dust, hands on his thighs, looking down now

voice_offers.read: He's silent; press for the fact that organizes everything.
voice_offers.line: Her name, John. If you know it, say it. If you don't, say that.

voice_offers.read: He's in the dust and failing; the cut is done.
voice_offers.line: Okay. Okay. Take a breath. I'm not going anywhere.

player_utterance: You already gave me the word. You said it yourself — cowardice on the inside, unanswerable in a church basement. So stop reaching for discretion and let that one stand. It's the first honest thing you've said that didn't cost you someone else's language.

believer_state_after.posture: still on his knees in the dust, but his shoulders have settled, his hands lifting off his thighs

voice_offers.read: He's flinching from the word; force him to own it.
voice_offers.line: Say it, John. The word. If it isn't cowardice, give me the better one — right now.

voice_offers.read: He's breaking; the word doesn't matter anymore.
voice_offers.line: John — you don't have to find the word. I see you. Just breathe.
